perturbation.original_text: This obligation shall be binding on all employees, offic ers, subsidiaries, affiliates or successors of the Receiving Party and shall continue for a period of five years from the date that the Confidential Information is provided to the Receivi ng Party, regardless of whether the Agreement has been terminated.
perturbation.changed_text: This obligation shall be binding on all employees, offic ers, subsidiaries, affiliates or successors of the Receiving Party and shall continue for a period of six months from the date that the Confidential Information is provided to the Receivi ng Party, regardless of whether the Agreement has been terminated.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the confidentiality period to six months, which may be unreasonably short depending on the nature of the confidential information and industry standards. This could be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets under applicable laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) aims to protect trade secrets for a reasonable period. A six-month confidentiality period might be challenged as inadequate to safeguard trade secrets effectively, conflicting with the intent of UTSA. The law doesn't set a specific time, but expects that it will be a 'reasonable' time.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from five years to six months. The Law Explanation states that the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) seeks to protect trade secrets for a "reasonable period", and a six-month period might be challenged as inadequate. This creates an inconsistency between the original contract and the modified contract, which could be seen as conflicting with the intent of the UTSA by not reasonably protecting trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets, reinforcing the legal basis for safeguarding such information, though it doesn't specify a timeframe. This indicates the modified contract, by reducing the period to six months, might not provide reasonable protection, potentially conflicting with legal expectations for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if so required by judicial or administrative proces s, provided that it shall promptly notify the Disclosing Party so as to allow the Disclosing Part y reasonable time to oppose such process, and provided that Receiving Party shall minimize su ch disclosure to the extent possible.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if so required by judicial or administrative proces s, and the Receiving Party is not required to notify the Disclosing Party of such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for prompt notification to the Disclosing Party impairs their ability to take legal action to protect their confidential information, potentially violating due process and trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45 dictates procedures for subpoenas, including those related to confidential information. By removing the notification requirement, the Receiving Party might not provide the Disclosing Party with sufficient time to object to a subpoena seeking confidential information, potentially violating due process rights and the ability to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the Receiving Party to notify the Disclosing Party when confidential information is sought through legal processes. This directly impairs the Disclosing Party's ability to protect their confidential information, which may be considered a trade secret. The Law Explanation states that 'By removing the notification requirement, the Receiving Party might not provide the Disclosing Party with sufficient time to object to a subpoena seeking confidential information, potentially violating due process rights and the ability to protect trade secrets.' Although the scraped snippets focus on the mechanics of subpoenas (Rule 45), the removal of notification directly impacts the due process rights of the disclosing party, which are implicitly protected by the legal system. The change creates a significant legal contradiction because it deprives a party of the opportunity to defend its interests in a legal proceeding.

perturbation.original_text: Upon such termination or other expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will return all of the Confidential Information in written or other tangible form, including any copies made, to the Disclosing Party together with certification that any other copies or notes or summaries (to the extent i ncluding the Confidential Information) have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon such termination or other expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party is not required to return the confidential information in written or other tangible form.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the obligation to return confidential information after termination, the agreement fails to protect the Disclosing Party's proprietary rights, potentially enabling the Receiving Party to misuse trade secrets in violation of intellectual property laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA aims to protect trade secrets, and not requiring the return of confidential information upon termination significantly increases the risk of misappropriation. This directly undermines the protections intended by trade secret laws, as the Receiving Party may retain and potentially misuse the information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the intent of trade secret law. The original text required the return of confidential information upon termination, which is a standard practice to protect trade secrets. The changed text explicitly states that the Receiving Party is not required to return confidential information, increasing the risk of misappropriation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1836) allows an owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated to bring a civil action. By removing the requirement to return confidential information, the contract significantly increases the likelihood of misappropriation, making it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to protect their trade secrets and enforce their rights under 18 USC 1836.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time by written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time with no notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement of written notice for termination could be problematic under contract law. Under general contract law, a lack of any notice can be seen as a breach of good faith and fair dealing, especially if the termination causes significant harm to the other party.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 implies that all contracts have an obligation of good faith, and lack of notice when terminating a contract may not align with that obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement of written notice for contract termination, which contradicts the implied obligation of good faith under UCC § 1-304. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." Removing the notice requirement directly undermines this obligation, as it can lead to unfair or detrimental outcomes for the other party, demonstrating a lack of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England without reference to the principles of conflict of laws
perturbation.changed_text: this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of North Korea without reference to the principles of conflict of laws
perturbation.explanation: Choosing North Korean law might be seen as unenforceable because North Korea's legal system lacks transparency and fairness, and the selection could be considered against public policy, especially if one party is based in a country with vastly different legal standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 allows parties to choose the governing law, but an exception is made if the chosen state has no reasonable relationship to the parties or the contract, or if it violates a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state. Selecting North Korean law, given its limited connection to the contract and potentially unfair legal system, could be seen as violating public policy, making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the contract is governed by the laws of England. The changed text replaces this with the laws of North Korea. The Perturbation Explanation states that North Korea's legal system lacks transparency and fairness, and the selection could be against public policy. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that choosing a state with no reasonable relationship to the parties or the contract, or that violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest, could make the clause unenforceable. While the scraped snippets do not directly address the validity of choosing North Korean law, the explanations provided, combined with the drastic shift in governing law, suggest a strong contradiction. Specifically, the altered text undermines the implicit requirement of a fair and reasonable legal framework, and the change is severe, making the clause likely unenforceable due to public policy concerns, as explained.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall assign or otherwise transfer any of its rights or obligations under this Agreement to any third pa rty without the prior written consent of the other party
perturbation.changed_text: Either party can assign or otherwise transfer any of its rights or obligations under this Agreement to any third party, and the other party will automatically be bound to such assignment. Consent of the other party is not required for an assignment of either rights or obligations.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement of consent for assignment contradicts standard contract law principles. Most jurisdictions require consent to assignment if the contract involves personal services or a relationship of trust and confidence.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-210 deals with assignment of rights and delegation of performance. While it allows for assignment, it also includes provisions that may restrict assignment if it would materially change the duty of the other party, increase the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract, or impair his chance of obtaining return performance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-210. Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-210. Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights. (1) A party may perform his duty through a delegate unless otherwise agreed or unless the other party has a substantial interest in having his original promisor perform or control the acts required by the contract . No delegation of performance relieves the party delegating of any duty to perform or any liability for breach. (2) Unless otherwise agreed all rights of either seller or buyer can be assigned except where the assignment would materially change the duty of the other party, or increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract , or impair materially his chance of obtaining return performance. A right to damages for breach of the whole contract or a right arising out of the assignor's due performance of his entire obligation can be assigned despite agreement otherwise. (3)Unless the circumstances indicate the contrary a prohibition of assignment of "the contract" is to be construed as barring only the delegation to the assignee of the assignor's performance. (4) An assignment of "the contract" or of "all my rights under the contract" or an assignment in similar general terms is an assignment of rights and unless the language or the circumstances (as in an assignment for security) indicate the contrary, it is a delegation of performance of the duties of the assignor and its acceptance by the assignee constitutes a promise by him to perform those duties. This promise is enforceable by either the assignor or the other party to the original contract . (5) The other party may treat any assignment which delegates performance as creating reasonable grounds for insecurity and may without prejudice to his rights against the assignor demand assurances from the assignee (Section 2-609 ). Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts standard contract law principles regarding assignment of rights and obligations. The original text requires prior written consent for any assignment, protecting each party's interests. The changed text removes this protection entirely, allowing either party to assign rights or obligations without the other's consent, which is not only a reversal of what was originally agreed upon, but can be a direct violation of UCC 2-210. UCC § 2-210(2) states that rights can be assigned unless the assignment would 'materially change the duty of the other party, or increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract , or impair materially his chance of obtaining return performance'. By removing the consent requirement, this creates a material change that the original text sought to prohibit.
